## **Adventures in the Office

The bedroom was dimly lit, a single candle flickering on the nightstand, casting soft shadows across the walls. Jeff leaned back against the headboard, watching Jodi as she unpinned her hair. Thirteen years together, and still, every little thing about her captivated him—the way she tucked a strand of hair behind her ear, the curve of her collarbone illuminated by the golden glow of the candlelight.  

Jodi caught him staring and smirked. "What are you looking at?"  

"You," he said, his voice warm and low, "you're beautiful."  

She laughed softly, the kind of laugh that only someone who knew him better than anyone else could give. "After all this time, you're still laying it on thick, huh?"  

Jeff didn’t answer, just slid closer to her. His hands found her waist, tugging her gently toward him until she was straddling his lap. His palms ran up her back, fingers tracing the familiar curve of her spine, and he tilted his head to look at her.  

"Not laying it on thick," he murmured, his lips brushing her jaw. "Just telling the truth."  

Jodi’s teasing expression softened, her breath hitching as his mouth moved to the sensitive spot just beneath her ear. She sighed, her hands resting on his shoulders, her fingers digging into the muscles there. "You always know how to get your way, don’t you?"  

He pulled back just enough to look her in the eyes, a playful glint in his. "Is it working?"  

She didn’t answer with words. Instead, she leaned down, kissing him deeply, her lips soft but demanding. Jeff groaned against her mouth, his hands sliding under her shirt, finding the warmth of her skin. The kiss deepened, years of familiarity blending with the thrill that never seemed to fade between them.  

Jeff shifted, gently lowering Jodi onto her back. He took his time, moving slowly, savoring every moment. His hands roamed over her body, rediscovering the contours he already knew by heart, while his lips mapped a path down her neck, her collarbone, and lower still.  

Jodi gasped, her head tipping back as he kissed the hollow between her ribs, his hands steady on her hips. "Jeff…" Her voice was breathless, a mix of surprise and anticipation.  

"Mm?" He glanced up, his grin both tender and mischievous. "Something you want to say?"  

She laughed softly, her fingers threading through his hair. "Just… don’t stop."  

"Wasn't planning on it."  

Jeff’s movements slowed, deliberate, his attention entirely on her. He wasn’t in a rush—he wanted to make her feel everything. The sound of her breath quickening, the way her body arched toward him—it was all he needed. Jodi’s fingers tightened in his hair as he continued, her whispered moans filling the room.  

When she finally fell apart beneath him, her body trembling, he stayed with her, holding her close, pressing kisses to her stomach and then her lips.  

"I love you," he murmured, his voice soft, reverent.  

Jodi smiled, pulling him down beside her, her cheeks flushed and her eyes shining. "I love you, too."  

Thirteen years together, and somehow, it still felt brand new.  

---    

The room was still, the air thick with warmth and the lingering scent of the candle on the nightstand. Jodi lay there, her body humming with aftershocks as Jeff hovered above her, his fingertips tracing light patterns over her bare skin. It wasn’t rushed—nothing ever was between them anymore. After thirteen years, they didn’t need urgency. They had time, and they knew how to savor it.

Jodi turned her head, her fingers brushing over his jaw, drawing him closer. His beard was soft under her touch, a comforting familiarity. “You’re looking at me again,” she teased, her voice soft but still laced with a playful edge.

Jeff’s lips curved into a slow smile. “Can you blame me?”

She arched a brow, but the blush on her cheeks gave her away. Even after all this time, he could make her feel like the only woman in the world. She tugged him down to her, their lips meeting again, slower this time—deeper. The kind of kiss that wasn’t just about desire, but about everything they’d built together over the years. The arguments, the laughter, the quiet moments, the love that had only grown stronger.

Jeff’s hand slid up her thigh, his touch firm yet tender, and Jodi let out a soft sigh against his mouth. She shifted beneath him, her legs wrapping around his waist as she pulled him closer. The heat between them was magnetic, pulling them together like it always did. His weight pressed against her in just the right way, and she felt herself melt even further into the mattress.

“You’re not going anywhere tonight, are you?” she murmured, her fingers tangling in the hair at the nape of his neck.

Jeff chuckled, low and rough, his breath warm against her skin. “Not a chance.”

He kissed her again, and this time, it was hungrier. The kind of kiss that made her heart race even though she knew exactly what was coming next. His hands slid under her, lifting her slightly, and she let herself go completely, surrendering to him in a way that felt as natural as breathing.

Every touch, every movement, was a testament to how well they knew each other’s bodies. He didn’t have to guess where to kiss, where to touch—he already knew. And Jodi responded instinctively, her body arching into him, her nails digging lightly into his shoulders as his lips found her neck again. She gasped, her breath catching as he moved lower, teasing her in ways that sent shivers racing down her spine.

“Jeff…” Her voice was breathless, filled with both need and affection.

He lifted his head, his gaze locking with hers. His eyes were dark, filled with that intensity she loved so much, but there was something else there, too—something softer. “I’ve got you,” he murmured, his voice like a promise.

And he did.  

As the night stretched on, they moved together, slow and deliberate at first, then faster, more desperate, their bodies syncing in a rhythm that spoke of years of connection. It wasn’t just about the pleasure—it was about them. About the way they fit together, both physically and emotionally. Every kiss, every whisper, every breathless moan was a reminder of the love they’d built over more than a decade.

When they finally collapsed into each other, their bodies tangled, the room quiet once more except for the sound of their breathing, Jodi let out a contented sigh. Jeff’s arm draped over her, pulling her close, and she nestled against his chest.

“You’re still my favorite person,” she whispered, her voice drowsy.

Jeff chuckled, pressing a kiss to the top of her head. “Good. Because you’re stuck with me.”

She smiled, her eyes closing as she let herself drift off, safe in his arms. Thirteen years, and it still felt like magic.  

---

The room was quiet now, the faint chirping of crickets filtering through the open window. The moonlight spilled in, soft and silvery, casting everything in a glow that made it feel like time had stopped. Jeff lay on his side, propped up on one elbow, his eyes fixed on Jodi as she rested beside him, her chest rising and falling in a slow, content rhythm.

The moonlight touched her skin, highlighting the curve of her shoulder and the delicate slope of her neck. It shimmered against her hair, loose now, fanned out across the pillow in a way that reminded him of the waves on the lake where they'd once spent an anniversary—wild, untamed, and breathtaking. Her lips, still flushed and slightly parted, tempted him to lean down and kiss her again, but he stayed still, caught in the quiet beauty of her.

He didn’t say a word, didn’t dare break the moment. Instead, he let himself study her, really look at her. The faint lines at the corners of her eyes, proof of every smile they’d shared. The little freckle on her collarbone he’d kissed a thousand times. The way her body softened into the bed, completely at ease, as if she trusted him to hold the world at bay while she rested.

Thirteen years together, and she still took his breath away.  

The moonlight made her look almost otherworldly, like a painting brought to life. But what held him the most was the way she was still so utterly Jodi. The woman who laughed too loud at her own jokes. The one who danced barefoot in the kitchen when her favorite song came on. The one who had stood by him through every high and every low, never once wavering.

His heart swelled, the kind of love he hadn’t thought possible before her filling every corner of his chest. He reached out, his fingers brushing lightly over her arm, and she stirred, her lips curling into a sleepy smile as her eyes fluttered open.

“You’re staring again,” she murmured, her voice thick with drowsiness.

Jeff smiled, his thumb gently tracing the curve of her jaw. “Can you blame me?” he echoed, his voice low and filled with awe.

Jodi chuckled softly, her hand finding his, squeezing it lightly. “You’re such a sap.”

“Only for you,” he whispered, leaning down to press a kiss to her forehead.

The moonlight continued to bathe them in its glow, but Jeff didn’t need it to see her beauty. He saw it in every glance, every laugh, every quiet moment like this. She was his home, his everything—and in this light, she was nothing short of a miracle.

---  

The bedroom was quiet except for the soft hum of the evening—a distant car passing, the faint rustle of leaves outside the window. Jeff leaned against the headboard, his shirt discarded, watching Jodi as she sat across from him on the edge of the bed. The candlelight flickered between them, casting a warm, golden glow over her skin.  

"You trust me, right?" Jeff asked softly, his voice steady but tinged with anticipation.  

Jodi tilted her head, a small smile playing on her lips. "After all this time, you’re seriously asking that?"  

He grinned, leaning forward to rest his hands on her thighs, his thumbs brushing her skin. "Just wanted to make sure."  

There was a moment of quiet between them, the kind of quiet that spoke volumes. Jodi studied his face, the way his eyes held hers with that steady, unwavering focus. She could feel her cheeks warm, but it wasn’t from embarrassment—it was the kind of heat that came from being seen, truly seen.  

"What are you thinking?" she asked, her voice barely above a whisper.  

Jeff hesitated, not because he was unsure, but because he wanted to say it right. "I want to know what you feel," he said finally. "I want to hear you tell me. No filters. Just you."  

Jodi’s breath caught slightly, her lips parting as she processed his words. It wasn’t the first time he’d asked her to step outside of her comfort zone—they’d grown together by exploring these vulnerable spaces—but still, there was a thrill in the way he asked. It wasn’t a demand, not even a request. It was an invitation.  

"And you’ll just sit there and watch?" she teased, trying to lighten the intensity of the moment.  

Jeff chuckled, his hand slipping down to give her knee a playful squeeze. "You know I will."  

Her smile lingered, but it was softer now, her heartbeat quickening as she leaned back onto the pillows. She closed her eyes for a moment, exhaling slowly, letting herself sink into the moment. Jeff stayed where he was, giving her the space to take the lead, his gaze never leaving her.

Her fingers slid along her own skin, hesitant at first, but the warmth of his presence grounded her. "It feels…" she began, her voice trailing off as she sought the right words.  

"Take your time," Jeff murmured.  

"It feels like… anticipation," she said finally, her hands exploring with slow, deliberate movements. "Like my whole body is waking up, but… only just barely."  

Jeff shifted slightly, leaning closer but still keeping his distance. "Go on," he encouraged, his voice low and soothing.  

Her breath hitched as her touch became bolder, her body responding in ways that felt both familiar and new under the weight of his gaze. "It’s like… heat," she whispered, her voice trembling slightly. "Like it’s building, but I don’t know where it’s going to go. Like it’s—"  

She stopped, her cheeks flushing as she opened her eyes and met his.  

"Keep going," he said gently, his expression soft, without a hint of judgment.  

Jodi hesitated for just a moment before her lips curled into a small, mischievous smile. "You’re enjoying this, aren’t you?"  

"More than you know," Jeff admitted, his voice tinged with warmth and desire. "But I think you are, too."  

Her laughter was quiet, but it filled the room, cutting through the tension just enough to make her feel completely at ease. And as the night unfolded, Jodi found herself letting go, her words flowing as freely as her emotions, their connection deepening in a way that reminded her why, after all these years, Jeff was still the only person she’d ever bare herself to—mind, body, and soul.  

---

Jodi’s smile turned playful, her confidence growing as she shifted against the pillows. “You know,” she murmured, her voice dripping with a mix of teasing and affection, “it’s kind of adorable how much you still look at me like this after all these years. You never get tired of me, do you?”

Jeff chuckled, the sound low and rich as he dragged a hand through his hair, his eyes locked on her like she was the only thing that mattered. “Not even for a second.”  

Her cheeks flushed, but it wasn’t shyness—it was the thrill of being the center of his attention, the way his gaze made her feel like the most beautiful woman in the world. “I think I kind of like being in the spotlight,” she admitted, her fingers tracing slow circles against her own skin, drawing his gaze lower.  

Jeff leaned forward, resting his elbows on his knees as he watched her, his breath hitching slightly. “Yeah? You’re glowing right now.” His voice was softer, but it carried that edge of heat that sent a shiver racing down her spine.

Jodi’s movements slowed for just a moment as she glanced up at him, her lips curving into a mischievous smile. “And what about you?” she asked, her voice lower now, the teasing edge replaced by something sultrier. “Do you want to do anything in return?”  

That caught him off guard. Jeff blinked, his usual confidence faltering for a moment. “Uh…” He laughed awkwardly, scratching the back of his neck. “I didn’t really think that far ahead.”  

Jodi arched a brow, delighted by the way she could still fluster him after all this time. “Come on, Jeff,” she teased, her tone light but laced with a growing hunger. “I’m over here baring it all—literally—and you’re just sitting there. What do you want?”  

Jeff hesitated, his eyes darting over her body as if searching for the right answer. “I mean… I want to keep watching you, for one,” he admitted, his voice growing rougher. “But if you’ve got other ideas…”  

Jodi let out a breathy laugh, shifting again, her legs tangling in the sheets as her confidence surged. “Options, huh? Let me help you out, babe.”  

She leaned forward slightly, her voice dropping as her words came out slower, more deliberate. “You could touch me,” she offered, her hand sliding up her thigh in a way that made his jaw tighten. “Or maybe… I touch you instead?” She tilted her head, her gaze flicking to him, her eyes filled with equal parts love and mischief. “Or both.”  

Jeff’s mouth went dry. “Both?” he repeated, his voice strained.

“Both,” she confirmed, leaning back with a satisfied smirk. “But since you’re giving me options, I guess it’s only fair that I get to choose what happens first.”  

He exhaled a shaky laugh, shaking his head. “You’re going to drive me crazy, you know that?”  

Jodi grinned, her eyes sparkling. “Good. That’s the whole point.”  

She reached for his hand, guiding it toward her as her smile softened, her tone shifting again. “I love this, you know. The way you look at me. The way you still want me.” Her voice broke slightly, her vulnerability shining through despite her playful tone. “It’s like… it makes me want to give you everything.”  

Jeff’s heart swelled at her words, and his hand tightened over hers instinctively. “You already do,” he said quietly, his voice filled with reverence.  

And as the night stretched on, they explored those options together, laughing, touching, and loving in a way that felt fresh and new despite all their years together. It wasn’t just about the heat—it was about the connection, the joy of still surprising each other, and the thrill of knowing they’d built something that would never fade.
 
Jeff’s breathing was uneven now, his body tense with anticipation as Jodi leaned closer, her eyes sparkling with the kind of mischief he couldn’t resist. She was glowing—not just from the candlelight but from the thrill of being in control, of having his full attention. After thirteen years, she knew exactly how to push his buttons, and she loved every second of it.

“You’re really something else, you know that?” Jeff muttered, his voice thick, almost a growl as his hand dragged through his hair.

Jodi smirked, settling herself beside him with an air of playful confidence. “I mean, I’ve had a pretty solid track record keeping you happy for this long,” she teased, her tone light but filled with pride. Her fingers traced the edge of his stomach, deliberate and slow, drawing a shudder from him.

Jeff leaned his head back against the headboard, closing his eyes for a moment, trying to steady himself. “You’re gonna be the death of me.”

“Stop being dramatic,” she replied with a laugh, shifting to straddle him. Her hands moved to his chest, resting there for just a moment before sliding downward, her touch sending sparks of heat radiating through him.  

He looked up at her, eyes dark with desire but edged with that familiar softness that always managed to ground her. “Jodi…” he started, his voice breaking slightly as she moved against him.

She tilted her head, her smile widening. “What?”  

“I’m just saying…” He exhaled, shaking his head with a breathy laugh. “If you’re planning what I think you’re planning, it’s going to be… messy.”

Jodi rolled her eyes, leaning down until their faces were just inches apart. “Jeff,” she murmured, her voice lower now, almost a purr. “I’ve been dealing with *your messes* for thirteen years. I think I can handle this one.”

His lips twitched into a grin, but his breath hitched when she pulled back slightly, her hands sliding lower.  

“Besides,” she continued, her tone sweet but with a wicked edge, “you don’t have to worry about anything you can’t touch.”  

That did it. Whatever semblance of composure Jeff had been clinging to vanished, replaced by a groan that rumbled deep in his chest. “You’re impossible,” he muttered, his hands gripping her hips, unsure whether he wanted to slow her down or pull her closer.

Jodi leaned into him, her lips brushing his ear as her fingers continued their work, teasing and deliberate. “And yet, here you are,” she whispered, her breath warm against his skin.

Jeff’s laugh was strained, cut off by another sharp intake of breath as she shifted again, this time bringing both hands into play. She moved with precision, her confidence radiating in every motion, every touch.  

Her eyes locked onto his, her smile softening as she watched the way his head tipped back, his body tense under her touch. She loved this—loved seeing the way she could unravel him, how he never held back with her.  

It didn’t take long before his grip on her hips tightened, his breathing growing ragged. “Jodi, I—”

“Shh,” she interrupted, her smile turning tender. “I’ve got you.”

And she did.  

When he finally let go, his body shuddering beneath her, Jodi held steady, her hands still moving gently as she guided him through it. The room was filled with the sound of his labored breathing, the tension in his body slowly melting away as he slumped back against the headboard.

She sat back, her own breathing uneven but filled with laughter as she grabbed a nearby towel from the nightstand—a precaution she’d learned to keep on hand over the years. “See? Nothing I can’t handle,” she teased, tossing the towel at him as she leaned in to kiss his cheek.  

Jeff let out a weak laugh, still catching his breath. “I don’t deserve you.”  

Jodi smirked, settling against his chest, her head resting just under his chin. “No, you don’t,” she quipped, her voice light, though the way she nuzzled closer to him spoke of the depth of her affection.  

They stayed like that for a while, tangled together, the warmth of their connection wrapping around them like a blanket. Thirteen years, and somehow, they were still finding new ways to surprise each other.  
  
The candle on the nightstand flickered low, its flame a soft, warm heartbeat in the quiet room. Jeff and Jodi lay tangled together under the sheets, her head resting on his chest, his arm draped protectively around her. The night had slowed to an easy rhythm, their breathing in sync, the weight of their earlier passion giving way to a calm, comfortable silence.  

Jodi sighed contentedly, trailing her fingers absentmindedly over his stomach. “You’re awfully quiet,” she murmured, her voice soft, teasing.  

Jeff chuckled, his chest rumbling under her ear. “Just… thinking,” he replied, his hand moving to tuck a strand of her hair behind her ear.  

She tilted her head up, her cheek resting against him as she smiled. “Thinking about what?”  

He hesitated, his lips twitching into a small grin. “How you’re always two steps ahead of me,” he admitted, his voice warm with affection. “I think you’ve been playing chess, and I’ve been playing checkers for the past thirteen years.”  

Jodi laughed softly, the sound light and musical. “That’s because I’m a genius, obviously,” she quipped, pressing a quick kiss to his collarbone. “You’re lucky I keep you around.”  

“Lucky doesn’t even begin to cover it,” he said, his voice low but earnest.  

She smiled again, shifting slightly to snuggle closer, her hand resting on his chest. For a while, they didn’t say anything, letting the quiet settle over them. The only sounds were the faint rustle of sheets when one of them shifted and the occasional sigh of contentment.  

Jeff’s hand moved in lazy circles on her back, his fingers brushing along the curve of her spine. The motion was soothing, and Jodi felt herself sinking deeper into his warmth. “I could stay like this forever,” she murmured, her eyes fluttering closed.

“Me too,” Jeff said softly, his voice almost a whisper.

As the minutes passed, they drifted into idle conversation, the kind that only came naturally to two people who knew each other inside and out. They talked about nothing and everything—work, funny moments from the week, a stray memory from their first year together that made them both laugh.  

At one point, Jeff shifted slightly, his arm tightening around her as his body stirred. Jodi noticed, of course, but she didn’t say anything at first. Instead, she smirked to herself, enjoying the way he tried to play it cool.  

After a moment, she tilted her head up to look at him. “You know, it’s really cute how that just… happens,” she teased, her eyes sparkling.  

Jeff groaned, covering his face with his free hand. “I can’t help it,” he mumbled. “You’re right here, and…” He trailed off, his cheeks flushing slightly.  

Jodi grinned, pressing a kiss to his chest. “It’s fine,” she said, her tone playful but reassuring. “I’d be offended if it didn’t.”  

He laughed, his hand moving to run through her hair. “You make it really hard to relax, you know that?”  

“Do I?” she asked innocently, though the glint in her eyes gave her away.  

They fell into another bout of comfortable silence, Jodi’s fingers tracing lazy patterns on his chest while his hand rested on her back. Every now and then, his body responded to her closeness, and she’d grin, amused at how easily she could get to him even in the quietest moments. But neither of them made a move to take things further—it wasn’t about that now.  

Instead, they stayed like that, wrapped up in each other, whispering and laughing quietly as the night stretched on. The intimacy wasn’t just physical—it was in the way they looked at each other, the way they touched without thinking, the way they felt completely at ease in each other’s arms.

As the candle finally burned out, plunging the room into soft darkness, Jodi sighed again, her voice drowsy as she spoke. “You’re still my favorite person, you know.”  

Jeff smiled, pressing a kiss to the top of her head. “Right back at you, babe.”  

And for the next hour, they stayed just like that—cuddling, kissing, and chatting in soft whispers, the love between them as steady and comforting as the rhythm of their hearts.  
  
---

Jodi stirred slightly, her cheek pressed against Jeff’s chest as she let out a soft, satisfied sigh. The quiet of the room made everything feel weightless, like time had slowed down just for them. She shifted a little, the faint grin on her lips impossible to hide as she glanced down and noticed Jeff’s body betraying him once again.

“You’re… persistent, aren’t you?” she teased, her voice sleepy but amused.  

Jeff groaned softly, letting his head fall back against the headboard. “Can you blame me?” he replied, his hand lazily tracing along her arm. “You’re lying here naked, all warm and soft. What am I supposed to do? Just… ignore that?”  

Jodi laughed, her fingers brushing lightly over his chest. “You could try.”  

“I don’t think I can,” he admitted, his voice tinged with humor but underlined by sincerity.  

Her laughter faded into a thoughtful hum as she tilted her head to look up at him. “Well,” she said, her voice slow and deliberate, “how about I deal with it first thing in the morning? That sound okay to you?”  

Jeff let out a slow exhale, his hands resting on her waist as he smiled down at her. “I mean… I’d prefer not to wait,” he said, his grin widening as he caught the playful glint in her eyes. “But if you’re calling the shots, I can survive ‘til morning.”  

“Good,” she said, patting his chest as if to settle the matter. “It’s Monday, after all. Gotta pace ourselves.”  

Jeff chuckled, shaking his head. “I don’t know how you stay so calm about this,” he said, his tone light but tinged with amazement. “You’re naked, Jodi. Naked. How am I supposed to turn that off?”  

Her grin widened, but she tried to play it cool. “You just focus on… whatever it is guys focus on when they’re trying to cool down.”  

“Impossible,” Jeff said immediately, his voice dropping a little as he looked at her. “It’s not just when you’re naked. It’s when you’re dressed, too. Hell, it’s even when you’re wearing those old sweatpants with the hole in the knee.”  

Jodi blinked, momentarily disarmed by the way he said it—not joking, not teasing, but with that earnest warmth that always got to her.  

“You’re hopeless,” she muttered, her cheeks flushing as she buried her face in his chest to hide her smile.  

Jeff smiled down at her, his hand brushing over her hair. “Hopelessly in love, maybe,” he said softly.  

She looked up at him again, her expression softening as she studied his face. There it was—that tenderness she could never quite get used to, the way he always made her feel like she was the most incredible person in the world.  

“You’re lucky you’re cute,” she murmured, her voice tinged with affection as she leaned up to kiss him.  

“I know,” he whispered against her lips, his arms tightening around her as they sank back into the pillows, their laughter fading into the quiet hum of the night.  

---

The room was still, the faint glow of the moonlight filtering through the curtains casting soft shadows across the bed. Jeff had fallen asleep not long after their whispered conversation, his arm still loosely draped around her waist, his breathing slow and steady.  

Jodi lay there beside him, her head resting on the pillow as her gaze traced the ceiling. She was wide awake, her mind swirling with thoughts as she listened to the rhythm of his breaths.  

Thirteen years together, and still, Jeff’s relentless desire for her caught her off guard sometimes. Not just the physical part—though that was certainly part of it—but the way he seemed completely, unwaveringly captivated by her. Even now.  

She glanced at him, her lips curving into a soft smile. His features were relaxed in sleep, his mouth slightly parted, the faintest trace of stubble catching the light. He looked peaceful, like he didn’t have a care in the world.  

Jodi exhaled quietly, turning her head to watch him. Her fingers brushed lightly over his arm, the same arm that had held her so tightly just hours ago. It was hard to wrap her head around it sometimes—the way he seemed to light up just by being near her, the way he wanted her with such intensity after all these years.  

*Relentless,* she thought, the word circling in her mind. That’s what he was. Not in a demanding way, never that. But in the way he looked at her like she was the only thing in the world that mattered. In the way he couldn’t seem to keep his hands off her, even when they were doing something as mundane as watching TV. In the way his eyes darkened with desire when she so much as walked into a room.  

And tonight… tonight had been no different. The way he’d unraveled under her touch, the way he’d whispered her name like it was a prayer—it still made her heart race just thinking about it.  

Jodi bit her lip, her gaze drifting back to the ceiling as her thoughts deepened. She’d never struggled to show her love for Jeff—she adored him, and she knew he felt it in every kiss, every laugh, every touch. But his relentlessness, his sheer intensity when it came to her… sometimes it felt like something she couldn’t fully relate to.  

How did he never seem to tire of her? How could he still look at her like that after all this time, as though she were something new and thrilling?  

She let out a quiet breath, her lips curving into a small smile. Maybe it wasn’t something she needed to understand. Maybe it was just who Jeff was—the way he loved, the way he existed.  

And maybe she didn’t need to relate to it to appreciate it.  

Because even if she didn’t always understand the depth of his relentlessness, she knew one thing for sure: she felt it. In every kiss, in every touch, in every look he gave her, she felt it.  

Turning her head, she pressed a gentle kiss to his shoulder, her lips lingering there for a moment before she rested her head against his chest.  

“Goodnight, you ridiculous man,” she murmured softly, her words barely audible over the sound of his breathing.  

And as she closed her eyes, the thought settled in her mind like a warm, comforting truth: Jeff’s relentlessness wasn’t something to figure out. It was something to cherish.    

---

The room was quiet except for the faint rustle of sheets and the low hum of the night outside. Jeff stirred, blinking his eyes open to the soft shadows stretching across the ceiling. The warmth of Jodi’s body pressed against him was a welcome reminder of where he was, who he was with.  

And yet, his body had other ideas. Again.  

He sighed quietly, running a hand over his face as he shifted carefully, trying not to wake her. The familiar ache of arousal pulled at him, as relentless as ever. It was like his body didn’t understand the concept of rest, not when she was so close, her bare skin brushing against him.  

He glanced down at her, his heart squeezing at the sight. She was curled up against his side, her arm draped across his stomach, her head nestled into his chest. The sheets had slipped down, exposing the curve of her shoulder and part of her back, her skin glowing faintly in the moonlight.  

Jeff swallowed hard, his hand brushing over her arm as if to steady himself. She was stunning—*still* stunning after all these years. It didn’t matter that he’d seen her like this countless times. Every time felt like the first. The soft slope of her hips, the way her hair tumbled over her shoulders, the gentle rise and fall of her breath—it all stirred something deep inside him that he couldn’t explain, something primal and tender all at once.  

He shifted again, trying to ease the tension in his body, but it wasn’t just about that. It wasn’t just her body that made him feel this way, though it certainly didn’t hurt. It was *her*. The way she teased him. The way she smiled at him like he was the only one who could make her laugh the way he did. The way she always seemed to know when he needed her—whether it was a kiss, a laugh, or just her presence.  

Jeff let out a quiet breath, his mind wandering to the events of the day. He thought about how she’d teased him earlier, the way she’d laughed when she noticed his body reacting to her. The way she’d kissed him softly and told him to wait until morning, like it was the easiest thing in the world.  

He chuckled to himself, shaking his head. She had no idea, did she? No idea how completely she unraveled him. How just the thought of her could set him off, let alone the reality of her lying naked beside him.  

But it wasn’t just the physical side of things. That was part of it, sure, but the bigger picture—the life they’d built together—was what truly floored him. He thought about the years of hard work, the sacrifices they’d made, the arguments, the reconciliations, the laughter. He thought about how far they’d come, from the awkward first dates to the comfortable, passionate rhythm they’d found together now.  

And here he was, lying next to the woman of his dreams, her body pressed against his, her presence a constant reminder of everything he’d ever wanted.  

“God, I’m lucky,” he whispered to himself, his voice barely audible in the quiet room.  

He brushed a hand over her hair, his fingers lingering as he let himself sink into the moment. This was the life he’d worked for, the life he’d dreamed of. And even in moments like this—when his body wouldn’t let him rest, when the intensity of his feelings for her threatened to overwhelm him—he wouldn’t trade it for anything.  

Jeff sighed again, leaning down to press a soft kiss to her forehead. She stirred slightly, her lips curling into a faint smile as she nestled closer to him.  

“Go back to sleep,” she murmured drowsily, her voice barely a whisper.  

Jeff smiled, his hand resting on her back as he settled into the pillows. “I will,” he said softly. “Eventually.”  

And as he lay there, his thoughts full of her, Jeff couldn’t help but feel grateful—grateful for the life they’d built, for the love they shared, for the simple, overwhelming joy of waking up beside her every day.    
  
---

Jodi stirred awake, her eyes fluttering open to the soft gray of early morning light spilling through the curtains. For a moment, she lay still, letting the familiar warmth of Jeff’s body against hers keep her grounded. She sighed contentedly, her gaze drifting to the ceiling, where the tiny imperfections in the plaster created shapes and patterns she’d traced a hundred times before.  

Her mind was slow to focus, the haze of sleep still clinging to her. But then, something clicked. Jeff hadn’t woken her.  

She blinked, surprised at her own realization. Usually, she could count on him to stir in the middle of the night, driven by that relentless passion of his. Not that she minded most of the time. She liked the way he kissed her awake, the way his hands found her even in the dark. But last night… last night he’d let her rest, and she couldn’t help but feel grateful for it.  

Her lips curved into a small smile as she turned her head slightly, glancing down at him. His arm was draped possessively around her waist, his chest rising and falling in slow, steady breaths. He looked peaceful, but her eyes wandered lower, catching sight of the familiar telltale sign beneath the sheets.  

*Of course.*  

Jodi bit back a laugh, shaking her head as she turned her gaze back to the ceiling. *He really is inexhaustible,* she thought, her cheeks warming at the memory of their whispered promises the night before.  

Her mind drifted to the clock on her nightstand. She didn’t need to look to know the alarm was at least 40 minutes away—she had a knack for waking up just before it sounded. But that knowledge left her with a dilemma.  

She ran through her mental checklist for the morning. She had to get ready for work, obviously. Her morning skincare routine always took at least 10 minutes. Then there was the commute, which she could shave down if she left earlier than usual. And, of course, she *needed* to swing by Starbucks on the way. That vanilla latte wasn’t going to order itself.  

And then there was Jeff.  

Her eyes flicked back to him, her smile softening as her thoughts shifted to the promise she’d made. The blowjob she’d so casually mentioned last night had clearly stuck with him—his body was already more than ready, even while he slept. She ran a hand through her hair, debating how much time she could realistically allocate.  

Fifteen minutes? Twenty?  

Her lips quirked into a mischievous grin. Knowing Jeff, it might take less time than that—he had a way of unraveling quickly when she took control. But she wanted to do it right, wanted to savor the moment the way he always seemed to savor her.  

She exhaled slowly, weighing her options. Could she afford to drift back asleep for a few more minutes? Probably. But did she want to? That was another question entirely.  

Her hand brushed lightly over his arm, her fingers trailing down to rest against his chest. She felt the steady thrum of his heartbeat beneath her touch, a soothing reminder of everything they’d built together.  

“Guess I better get started,” she murmured to herself, a playful smile tugging at her lips.  

Turning toward him fully, she leaned in, her lips brushing against his ear. “Hey,” she whispered, her voice soft but teasing. “Morning, sleepyhead. I’ve got a promise to keep.”  

 ---

Jodi leaned in closer, her lips brushing against Jeff’s ear as her voice softened. “Hey,” she whispered, drawing the word out just enough to stir him from his sleep.  

Jeff grumbled something unintelligible, his arms tightening reflexively around her waist as he shifted. His body stirred in more ways than one, and Jodi bit back a grin.  

“Wake up, babe,” she murmured, her hand sliding down his chest in a way that was far from accidental.  

Jeff’s eyes fluttered open, his brow furrowed in confusion for a brief moment before they focused on her face. When her playful smile registered, he let out a low groan. “What time is it?” he muttered, his voice still thick with sleep.  

“Time for me to keep my promise,” she said simply, her lips curling into a mischievous smirk.  

Jeff blinked, his expression shifting from confusion to realization, followed quickly by a crooked grin. “Oh,” he said, his voice warming. “You’re really starting the morning like this?”  

“Don’t act so surprised,” Jodi teased, shifting to straddle him. “You know I always keep my word.”  

Jeff chuckled, his hands instinctively finding her hips as he gazed up at her. “I wasn’t doubting you,” he said, his voice still laced with sleep, “but I wasn’t expecting this level of commitment before coffee.”  

Jodi rolled her eyes, leaning down to press a kiss to his lips. “Consider it my good deed for the day,” she whispered, her breath warm against his mouth.  

Before he could respond, she shifted lower, her hands trailing over his stomach with slow, deliberate movements. Jeff’s breath hitched, his body already responding to her touch.  

“Jodi…” he started, but his voice trailed off as she looked up at him, her eyes sparkling with mischief.  

“Relax,” she said softly, her voice carrying both affection and playful command. “We’ve got time.”  

And she meant it. She wasn’t in any rush, and she wanted to make sure Jeff felt every second of what she was about to give him.  

Her movements were slow and intentional, her touch light at first, teasing, as she traced her fingers along his skin. Jeff’s breathing grew heavier, his hands gripping the sheets as he fought to stay in control. But Jodi wasn’t making it easy for him.  

She glanced up at him again, smiling at the way his head tipped back, his lips parted in a silent groan. “You’re really having fun, aren’t you?” she teased, her voice low and sultry.  

Jeff managed a shaky laugh, his eyes meeting hers for a fleeting moment. “You have no idea,” he murmured, his voice rough with need.  

She continued with the same slow, deliberate pace, alternating between teasing and satisfying in a way that kept him on edge. She loved watching him like this—the way his body responded to her, the way his breathing hitched with every movement, the way he couldn’t seem to keep his hands from reaching for her, even as she kept him just out of reach.  

Minutes stretched into what felt like eternity, and Jodi took her time, drawing it out in a way that made Jeff’s entire body tense with anticipation. “Jodi,” he groaned again, his voice somewhere between pleading and amazed.  

“What?” she asked innocently, her eyes glinting with amusement. “Something wrong?”  

“Nothing’s wrong,” he said quickly, his voice strained. “But you’re… you’re killing me here.”  

She laughed softly, leaning up to press a kiss to his stomach before looking up at him again. “Good,” she said simply, her tone playful but affectionate. “You deserve it.”  

When he finally reached his peak, his body trembling beneath her, Jodi held him steady, her hands gentle as she guided him through it. She stayed close, pressing soft kisses to his skin as he caught his breath, his hands finally relaxing their grip on the sheets.  

“You okay there?” she asked, her voice light but tinged with affection as she settled beside him.  

Jeff let out a breathless laugh, his arm draping over her as he pulled her close. “You’re unbelievable,” he murmured, his voice still rough.  

“I know,” she teased, resting her head on his chest.  

They lay like that for a while, the morning sunlight creeping into the room as their breathing slowed. Jodi glanced at the clock, realizing she’d have to rush a bit to fit in her Starbucks run, but it was worth it.  

Because the way Jeff held her, his lips brushing her hair as he whispered a quiet “I love you,” reminded her that moments like this were what really mattered.    
    
---

Jodi practically burst through the office doors, her bag slung over one shoulder, a steaming cup of Starbucks in her hand, and her hair still slightly damp from the rushed shower. Her heels clicked rapidly against the tile floor as she made a beeline for her office, dodging a pair of coworkers who were chatting near the break room.  

She glanced at the clock on the wall as she passed—it read 7:59:30. *Thirty seconds. Nailed it.*  

Sliding into her office, Jodi let out a breath, her cheeks flushed from her sprint from the parking lot. She set her coffee on her desk and flopped into her chair, letting her head fall back for a moment. The adrenaline from her mad dash still coursed through her veins, but she allowed herself a small smile. She’d made it.  

Seconds later, the familiar sound of footsteps echoed in the hallway, and two of her coworkers, Tara and Mike, appeared in her doorway, their expressions a mix of amusement and mild concern.  

“You actually made it,” Tara said, crossing her arms as she leaned against the doorframe.  

“Barely,” Jodi replied, sitting up straight and tucking a stray strand of hair behind her ear. “I had… a busy morning.”  

“Busy?” Mike raised an eyebrow, smirking. “You texted me an hour ago saying you were leaving soon. Did you stop for coffee *and* a scenic detour?”  

Jodi picked up her Starbucks cup and raised it like a trophy. “This,” she said, “was non-negotiable. Don’t judge me.”  

Tara snorted. “We’re not judging. We were just starting to wonder if you were, you know… *alive.*”  

“Safe and sound,” Jodi assured them, taking a long sip of her latte. She didn’t mention the reason for her tight timing. *What would they say if they knew?* The thought made her smirk against the rim of her cup.  

“You look… flushed,” Mike observed, his smirk deepening. “Stressful commute?”  

Jodi shot him a look, though she couldn’t help the laugh that escaped. “Something like that.”  

Tara shook her head, though she was smiling. “Well, we’re glad you made it. Don’t forget the team meeting starts in ten.”  

Jodi groaned, sinking further into her chair. “I haven’t even opened my email yet.”  

“Better get to it,” Tara said, tapping the doorframe as she and Mike headed back to their desks.  

Left alone, Jodi let out a quiet laugh, shaking her head. She’d cut it close, sure, but it had been worth it. She could still feel the lingering warmth of Jeff’s kiss from earlier, the playful way he’d sent her off with a promise to “behave” until she got home.  

“Totally worth it,” she muttered to herself, turning to her computer and taking another sip of coffee as the clock struck 8:00 sharp.    
   
---  
The work center huddle had barely ended when Tara appeared in Jodi’s doorway again, leaning casually against the frame with a knowing smirk on her face. Jodi, busy reviewing the morning’s emails, barely looked up at first.  

“Rough morning, huh?” Tara asked, her voice laced with amusement.  

Jodi glanced up, narrowing her eyes slightly at Tara’s tone. “What’s that supposed to mean?” she asked, though she couldn’t help the small smile tugging at her lips.  

Tara arched an eyebrow, her smirk widening. “Oh, come on. Did Jeff spend the night?”  

Jodi froze, blinking in surprise before blurting out, “How can you tell?”  

Tara threw her head back with a laugh, stepping fully into the office and closing the door behind her. “Jodi, you’re *always* like this after he does. It’s like clockwork.”  

“Like what?” Jodi asked defensively, though her cheeks flushed as she set her coffee cup down.  

“Like *this*,” Tara said, motioning to her with a flourish. “The only time I ever see you sprinting into work last second is when you’re way behind schedule—and glowing like someone just handed you the winning lottery ticket.”  

Jodi groaned, dropping her head into her hands. “You’re ridiculous.”  

“Am I, though?” Tara teased, crossing her arms as she leaned against Jodi’s desk. “You’ve got that ‘I had a very good night’ energy. And don’t even try to deny it—it’s written all over your face.”  

Jodi peeked at her from between her fingers, her cheeks still warm. “I hate that you know me this well.”  

“I’m your work wife; it’s my job,” Tara quipped. “Now spill. Did he stay over?”  

Jodi sighed, leaning back in her chair with a faint smile. “Yeah, he did.”  

Tara grinned. “And judging by the fact that you *just* made it into the office, I’m guessing he didn’t let you sleep much, huh?”  

“Can we not?” Jodi said, covering her face with her hands again, though she couldn’t stop the laughter bubbling out.  

“Oh, we absolutely can,” Tara said, clearly enjoying herself. “You’ve got that post-Jeff glow, girl. It’s unmistakable.”  

Jodi groaned again, but this time it was accompanied by a laugh. “You’re the worst.”  

“Just telling it like it is,” Tara said with a shrug. “And honestly? Good for you. He’s clearly doing *something* right if you’re out here cutting it close for work.”  

Jodi rolled her eyes, though her smile gave her away. “I swear, one of these days, I’m going to stop oversharing with you.”  

“You say that every time,” Tara replied, grinning as she pushed off the desk and headed for the door. “Anyway, let me know if you need me to cover for you next time you’re running late. Or if Jeff keeps you up again.”  

Jodi grabbed a pen and lightly tossed it in Tara’s direction. “Get out of here!”  

Tara laughed as she dodged the pen, waving over her shoulder as she walked back to her desk. “You’re glowing, babe! Own it!”  

Left alone, Jodi shook her head, her cheeks still warm as she turned back to her computer. Tara wasn’t wrong, though. Jeff had a way of turning her world upside down—and she wouldn’t have it any other way.    
  
---

By midmorning, Jodi had finally settled into her workday, though it hadn’t been without its challenges. Between catching up on emails and juggling her morning tasks, she could feel the ripple effects of her frantic start to the day. But every time she glanced at her phone and saw Jeff’s last cheeky text—*Worth it, wasn’t it?*—she couldn’t help but grin.

Her office family, however, was having a *field day* with her rushed morning.  

“Jodi, I gotta ask,” Tara called from the cubicle next to hers. “How’s that shirt treating you?”  

Jodi blinked, glancing down at herself. “What do you mean?”  

Tara smirked as she leaned around the divider. “Oh, nothing. Just that you forgot to button the top two buttons. Trying out a new look, or was that part of the sprint out the door?”  

Jodi groaned, laughing as she reached up to fix it. “Okay, first of all, you could’ve told me this *two hours ago*.”  

“Where’s the fun in that?” Tara teased.  

“Hey, I noticed too,” Mike chimed in from across the room, leaning back in his chair. “I just thought maybe you were trying to distract your boss into forgetting you were late.”  

Jodi tossed a paperclip in his direction, laughing as he dodged it. “You’re all terrible,” she said, shaking her head.  

“Oh, we’re not done yet,” Tara said with a grin. “What’s going on with your shoes?”  

Jodi frowned, glancing down at her feet. “What do you mean?”  

“Are those even a pair?” Tara asked, leaning closer. “The left one looks… *different.*”  

Jodi leaned down to inspect them and groaned. Sure enough, she’d grabbed two nearly identical black heels—but one had a slightly taller heel than the other. She sat back up, throwing her hands in the air. “Okay, fine! Yes, I was in a rush this morning. Are you happy now?”  

“Thrilled,” Tara said, grinning. “You’re usually so put together. This is a *rare* day, and we have to savor it.”  

“She’s right,” Mike said, crossing his arms. “You’re like some mythical creature of organization. Seeing you show up with mismatched shoes? This is our Super Bowl.”  

Jodi couldn’t stop laughing now, her cheeks flushed as she shook her head. “Fine, laugh it up. But you know what? I still made it here on time.”  

“Barely,” Tara pointed out, arching a brow. “And clearly, it came at a price. Wanna tell us what—or who—was so distracting this morning?”  

Jodi rolled her eyes, though her grin didn’t falter. “You’re all too nosy for your own good, you know that?”  

“We’re family,” Mike said with a shrug. “It’s our job.”  

As the teasing continued through the morning, Jodi found herself enjoying it more than she expected. Her coworkers weren’t just poking fun—they were reminding her how much she was part of the team. And even though they didn’t know *exactly* why she’d been running behind this morning, she didn’t mind the subtle jokes about mismatched shoes and half-buttoned shirts.  

When lunchtime rolled around, Tara plopped into the chair across from Jodi’s desk, her expression still amused. “Okay, seriously,” she said, leaning forward. “You gotta tell me. Was it Jeff?”  

Jodi raised an eyebrow. “What makes you think that?”  

“Because the *only* thing that ever throws you off your game is that man,” Tara said, grinning. “So… was it worth it?”  

Jodi’s grin widened, her cheeks flushing slightly. “Oh, it was worth it.”  

Tara laughed, shaking her head. “Good. Because the next time you show up like this, we’re never letting you live it down.”  

“Noted,” Jodi said, laughing as she grabbed her bag.  

As she headed to lunch with Tara, her coworkers still cracking jokes as they passed, Jodi couldn’t help but feel lucky. Between her chaotic morning with Jeff and the ribbing from her office family, she realized she had exactly the kind of life she’d always dreamed of—messy, imperfect, and absolutely full of love.    
   
---

The hum of the microwave filled the break room as Jodi leaned against the counter, waiting for her lunch to heat up. Tara was beside her, unwrapping a sandwich from the deli downstairs, while Mike sat at the nearby table, scrolling through his phone.  

“So,” Tara began, her tone dripping with curiosity, “are you going to tell us what had you running around like a crazy person this morning, or do we just get to guess all day?”  

Jodi smirked, tapping the edge of her Starbucks cup. “Oh, I thought you’d figured it all out already,” she teased.  

“Hardly,” Mike chimed in, glancing up from his phone. “You’re too much of a pro to leave us with just mismatched shoes and unbuttoned shirts. What’d we miss?”  

Jodi chuckled, glancing at Tara. “Well, first off, I did double-check that I was wearing panty hose, so don’t even start with me on that one.”  

Tara laughed, pointing her sandwich at Jodi. “I was *so* close to asking this morning. You had that look—like you forgot something major.”  

“Yeah, well, I didn’t,” Jodi shot back, laughing. “But…” She paused for dramatic effect, glancing between them. “You missed the biggest tell from last night.”  

Tara’s eyes narrowed, her interest piqued. “Biggest tell? Oh, this I *have* to hear.”  

Mike leaned forward, setting his phone down. “Alright, hit us with it. What’d we miss?”  

Jodi took a slow sip of her coffee, drawing out the moment as Tara groaned. “Come *on,* Jodi. Don’t make us work for it.”  

“Okay, okay,” Jodi said, grinning. “I’ll give you a hint: it’s something you *didn’t* see.”  

Tara tilted her head, thinking for a moment. “Didn’t see… Was it your earrings? You always wear those little gold hoops. Did you forget them?”  

“Nope,” Jodi said, shaking her head. “Nice try, though.”  

“Your nails,” Mike guessed, glancing at her hands. “You usually have them done. Did you skip your polish this week?”  

“Wrong again,” Jodi replied, wiggling her fingers in front of him. “I’m still rocking my usual nude shade, thank you very much.”  

Tara squinted at her, her eyes scanning Jodi from head to toe like a detective solving a mystery. “Alright, I give up. What is it?”  

Jodi’s grin widened as she leaned closer, dropping her voice to a conspiratorial whisper. “I forgot to pack my lunch,” she said, holding up her microwavable meal with a flourish.  

Mike rolled his eyes, groaning. “Oh, come *on.* That’s not even a big tell.”  

“It is for me,” Jodi countered, laughing. “You know I meal prep like my life depends on it. When’s the last time you saw me running around with one of these?” She gestured to the plastic container she was heating, steam starting to escape from the lid.  

Tara shook her head, laughing. “So *that’s* why you’ve been borrowing creamer for your coffee all morning. You didn’t even have time to restock your desk stash, did you?”  

“Guilty,” Jodi admitted, grabbing a napkin.  

“Alright, I’ll give you that one,” Tara said, smirking. “But seriously, was it Jeff?”  

Jodi hesitated, her smile turning playful. “What do you think?”  

“Oh, it *was* Jeff,” Tara said, pointing a finger at her. “I knew it! You’ve been practically floating all morning. That man really does a number on you, doesn’t he?”  

Jodi laughed, shaking her head as the microwave beeped. “You have no idea,” she said, pulling her lunch out and setting it on the counter.  

“I don’t need the details,” Mike interjected, raising his hands in mock surrender. “Just don’t forget your shoes next time, alright?”  

Jodi threw a balled-up napkin at him, her laughter filling the break room as Tara joined in.  

“Fair warning, though,” Tara said, still grinning. “Next time, I’m checking your desk drawer first thing. If you’re out of snacks, I’ll *know* you had a wild night.”  

  
---

Tara leaned against the counter, watching Jodi as she stirred her lunch absentmindedly, her cheeks slightly flushed. There was a mischievous glint in Tara’s eyes, the kind that made Jodi immediately suspicious.  

“So,” Tara began casually, her tone dripping with curiosity, “are you planning to send Jeff a steamy photo or two before heading back to your desk? You’ve got the glow, might as well lean into it.”  

Jodi froze mid-stir, her lips parting slightly as she blinked at Tara. “What makes you think I’d do something like that?” she asked, though the grin tugging at the corners of her mouth gave her away.  

Tara raised an eyebrow. “Oh, please. You’re practically *radiating* ‘I’m thinking about my man right now’ energy. Don’t tell me it hasn’t crossed your mind.”  

Jodi laughed, shaking her head as she leaned back against the counter. “Fine, you got me. I’m *seriously* considering it,” she admitted, biting her lip.  

“I knew it!” Tara exclaimed, pointing a finger at her. “I can’t blame you, though. That man seems to have some kind of spell on you.”  

Jodi sighed, her smile softening as she glanced at her phone. “You have no idea,” she murmured. “The way things worked out last night… he just let me sleep.”  

Tara tilted her head, curious. “Wait, seriously? No middle-of-the-night shenanigans?”  

“None,” Jodi said, her tone tinged with both surprise and affection. “I was *exhausted*, and he could tell. So instead of waking me up like he usually does, he just held me and let me recharge.”  

Tara crossed her arms, a smirk playing on her lips. “And now that you’re fully recharged… you’re fully horny, aren’t you?”  

Jodi burst out laughing, her face turning red as she covered her face with her hands. “Why are you like this?”  

“Because I’m right,” Tara teased, nudging her with her elbow.  

Jodi peeked at her through her fingers, a sheepish grin spreading across her face. “Okay, fine. Yes, I’m *very*… let’s just say I’m feeling a little pent up.”  

Tara snorted. “A little?”  

“Alright, *a lot,*” Jodi admitted, laughing. “He just… I don’t know. There’s something about him. Even after all these years, the way he looks at me, the way he touches me—it’s like he’s never gotten used to me, you know? Like I’m still this big deal to him.”  

Tara’s smirk softened into a genuine smile. “That’s sweet,” she said. “Annoyingly sweet, but still sweet. I can see why you’re hooked.”  

Jodi nodded, her fingers tracing the edge of her phone as she debated her next move. “I mean, I’ve got a few minutes before lunch is over…”  

“Do it,” Tara said immediately, her grin returning.  

“What if someone sees?” Jodi asked, laughing nervously.  

“Then be discreet! Find a mirror or something. You’re resourceful,” Tara replied, waving a hand. “Besides, you owe him after that morning sendoff, don’t you?”  

Jodi rolled her eyes, though she couldn’t help the grin that spread across her face. “You’re a terrible influence.”  

“Thank you,” Tara said brightly. “Now, go make your man’s day before you go back to pretending to work.”  

Jodi shook her head, laughing as she grabbed her phone and headed for the hallway. “You’re the worst,” she called over her shoulder.  

“And yet you love me!” Tara shouted back, her laughter echoing through the break room.  

As Jodi ducked into the quiet privacy of the restroom, her heart raced with anticipation. She might have been blushing, but she couldn’t deny the excitement bubbling in her chest. Fully recharged and fully *motivated,* she tapped her phone camera open, smiling to herself.    
  
---  
Mike leaned back in his chair, craning his neck to watch Jodi disappear into the hallway, phone in hand and a suspiciously determined look on her face. He glanced at Tara, raising an eyebrow.  

“She’s not about to do what I *think* she’s about to do, is she?” Mike asked, his tone somewhere between amused and incredulous.  

Tara, still munching on her sandwich, gave him a pointed look. “Oh, she absolutely is,” she said matter-of-factly, brushing a crumb off her lap.  

Mike let out a low whistle, shaking his head. “Unreal. I mean, good for her, but still… bold move in the middle of the workday.”  

Tara smirked, setting her sandwich down. “If you had a man like Jeff, you’d understand,” she said with a shrug. “The guy’s got her wrapped around his finger in the best possible way. It’s cute, really.”  

Mike snorted, crossing his arms. “You’re acting like I don’t already have a great setup at home.”  

Tara raised an eyebrow, leaning back in her chair. “Oh, I’m not saying you don’t, but if you were a *good* office husband, you’d take notes. Jodi’s glowing today because Jeff knows how to pay attention. You, meanwhile, have probably forgotten your wife’s favorite coffee order by now.”  

Mike opened his mouth to protest but hesitated, clearly searching his brain. “It’s… uh, caramel something, right?”  

Tara threw him a look that could melt steel. “Wow. Just wow. *Caramel something?* That’s the best you’ve got?”  

“I’ll have you know I bring her coffee all the time,” Mike defended, though his tone lacked conviction.  

“Sure you do,” Tara said dryly, taking another bite of her sandwich. “Meanwhile, Jeff’s over there making sure Jodi practically floats into work every time they spend the night together. He’s putting in the effort.”  

Mike rubbed the back of his neck, looking sheepish. “Alright, fine. Maybe I could step it up a little.”  

“A *little*?” Tara echoed, arching an eyebrow. “Try a lot. And start by figuring out her actual Starbucks order before Valentine’s Day rolls around. You’re welcome.”  

Mike groaned, but there was a faint smile tugging at his lips. “You’re relentless, you know that?”  

“Just trying to save your marriage, buddy,” Tara said with a grin, popping the last bite of her sandwich into her mouth.  

As they both turned their attention back to their lunches, the sound of footsteps echoed down the hall. Moments later, Jodi reappeared, a satisfied smile on her face as she slipped back into the break room.  

“Everything okay?” Tara asked innocently, though the glint in her eyes gave her away.  

“Perfect,” Jodi replied, her cheeks faintly flushed as she grabbed her coffee.  

Mike raised his hands in mock surrender. “I don’t even want to know,” he said, shaking his head.  

Tara smirked, leaning closer to Jodi. “Ignore him,” she said. “He’s just jealous he doesn’t have your energy—or Jeff’s.”  

“Damn right I’m jealous,” Mike muttered, grabbing his lunch and heading for the door. “Caramel something. I got it.”  

Jodi burst out laughing as Tara gave Mike a thumbs-up. “Good luck, buddy,” Tara called after him, her laughter mingling with Jodi’s as the door swung shut.    
   
---  
Jodi plopped into the chair across from Tara’s desk, crossing her legs and taking a long sip of her Starbucks. Her cheeks still had the faintest hint of pink, and there was an unmistakable spring in her step as she made herself comfortable. Tara raised an eyebrow, her curiosity already piqued.  

“Alright, spill,” Tara said, leaning back in her chair with a smirk. “You’ve got that look again.”  

Jodi tilted her head, feigning innocence. “What look?”  

“The ‘I just did something scandalous and I’m thriving’ look,” Tara replied, grinning. “Come on, out with it. What’d you do this time?”  

Jodi hesitated for a split second before a mischievous smile spread across her face. “Okay, fine,” she said, leaning in closer. “I… might have filmed myself getting off and sent Jeff the recording.”  

Tara’s jaw dropped, and for a moment, she just stared at Jodi in disbelief. “Shut. Up,” she finally said, her voice low but full of awe.  

Jodi laughed, shrugging like it was no big deal. “What? He’s been driving me crazy all day, and I needed to even the playing field.”  

Tara shook her head, still staring at her friend. “You’re *unreal,* you know that? Like, the rest of us are over here struggling to send a flirty text, and you’re out here making *movies.*”  

Jodi grinned, taking another sip of her coffee. “What can I say? I’m inspired. And now that I’ve got that out of my system, I’m ready to crush my 2 PM presentation.”  

Tara snorted, rolling her eyes. “Oh, you’re definitely going to crush it. But…” She leaned forward, giving Jodi a once-over. “We might need to tidy you up a bit first. You look… how do I put this nicely? A little too much.”  

Jodi frowned, glancing down at herself. “What do you mean? I thought I looked fine!”  

“Oh, you look more than fine,” Tara said, grinning. “That’s the problem. You’ve got the ‘I just had the best time of my life’ glow, and it’s *a lot.*”  

Jodi groaned, covering her face with her hands. “Why are you like this?”  

“Because someone has to keep you humble,” Tara teased, grabbing a compact mirror from her desk and handing it to Jodi. “Here, take a look. Your cheeks are way too flushed, and your hair is giving, like, post-romp chaos vibes.”  

Jodi took the mirror, examining herself with a critical eye. “Okay, maybe you’re not wrong,” she admitted, smoothing her hair back and reaching for her lipstick to touch it up.  
“See? That’s what I’m here for,” Tara said, smirking. “Because while *I* think the glow is hilarious, I’m not sure the higher-ups would appreciate it.”  

Jodi laughed, setting the mirror down and fluffing her hair one last time. “Thanks, Tara. You’re the best.”  

“I know,” Tara said, leaning back in her chair. “Now go crush that presentation—and try not to think about Jeff too much while you’re at it.”  

“No promises,” Jodi said, winking as she stood up and headed back to her office.  

As she walked away, Tara called after her, “Just don’t send any *more* videos during the meeting!”  

Jodi’s laugh echoed down the hallway, leaving Tara shaking her head with a grin.    
  
---  
Jodi stood at the head of the conference room, her hands resting lightly on the polished wood of the table as she finished her final slide. The presentation had gone off without a hitch—her points were clear, her data airtight, and her delivery confident. The room had been engaged, nodding along as she spoke, but now, as the chairman of the board rose from his seat, all eyes turned to him.  

“Ms. Dearth,” the chairman began, his deep voice commanding but warm, “this was an exceptional presentation. Your analysis is thorough, and your recommendations are not only forward-thinking but practical. I’ll be recommending your plan during next week’s strategic review for capital spending in the upcoming year.”  

Jodi’s heart stopped for a moment, her breath catching as his words sank in. Her plan—months of late nights, early mornings, and endless revisions—was being given the green light. The chairman himself was going to champion it.  

She swallowed, forcing herself to remain calm, professional. “Thank you, Mr. Jennings,” she said, her voice steady despite the slight flutter in her chest. “I’m glad the board found the plan useful. It’s been a collaborative effort, and I appreciate the opportunity to present it.”  

Her words were measured, her tone neutral, but inside, she was about to burst. *Oh my God. Oh my God. He’s recommending my plan.*  

The chairman nodded, offering her a rare smile. “You’ve done excellent work, Ms. Dearth. Keep it up.”  

As the chairman exited the room, the rest of the board members began to gather their materials, murmuring to one another about the presentation. Jodi remained rooted in place for a moment, her fingers gripping the edge of the table as she processed what had just happened.  

*Don’t scream,* she told herself, taking a slow, deep breath. *Not yet. Not here.*  

“Great job, Jodi,” one of the directors said as they passed, giving her an approving nod.  

“Very impressive,” another added.  

Jodi thanked them politely, nodding and offering her trademark professional smile. But as soon as the room cleared out, leaving her alone, the smile transformed into a full-blown grin. She exhaled sharply, clutching the back of a chair for support as a giddy laugh escaped her.  

*I did it. Holy crap, I did it.*  

She glanced at her laptop, still displaying the final slide of her presentation, and allowed herself a moment of pride. All the stress, the self-doubt, the extra effort—it had been worth it. Her plan wasn’t just good; it was the new standard.  

Pulling herself together, Jodi gathered her materials and straightened her blazer. As she walked back toward her office, her steps were lighter, her head held high. She kept the professional mask on, but inside, she was practically doing cartwheels.  

By the time she reached her desk, her phone buzzed with a text from Tara: *How’d it go?*  

Jodi grinned as she typed back: *Absolutely crushed it.*  

And she had.    

---  
Jodi was sitting at her desk, still riding the high from her successful presentation, when Tara slipped into her office, closing the door behind her with exaggerated care. She leaned back against the door, arms crossed, a wide grin plastered on her face.  

“Alright,” Tara said, eyeing Jodi with mock suspicion. “You’ve got *that look* again. You’re even more flushed now than you were an hour ago, and that’s saying something.”  

Jodi looked up, blinking in surprise before narrowing her eyes playfully. “Tara, do you ever stop?”  

“Nope,” Tara replied cheerfully, pushing off the door and walking over to perch on the edge of Jodi’s desk. “Now, spill. How did it go? Did they love it?”  

Jodi’s lips twitched into a wide smile, her giddiness bubbling to the surface. “They didn’t just love it,” she said, leaning forward conspiratorially. “The *chairman* said he’s going to recommend it at the strategic review next week.”  

Tara gasped, her hands flying to her mouth. “Are you serious?”  

“Dead serious,” Jodi said, nodding emphatically. “He called it ‘exceptional’ and said my recommendations were forward-thinking *and* practical.”  

“Oh my God!” Tara squealed, grabbing Jodi by the shoulders and giving her a little shake. “You *crushed* it! I knew you would, but still—look at you, killing it out here!”  

Jodi laughed, her cheeks flushing even more as she leaned back in her chair. “I’m so happy I feel like I might explode,” she admitted, her voice tinged with disbelief. “All those late nights, all that second-guessing… it actually paid off.”  

Tara beamed at her, her pride for her friend obvious. “Of course it did. You’re a rock star, Jodi. They’d be crazy not to recognize it.”  

Jodi grinned, leaning forward and resting her elbows on the desk. “It’s just—ugh, it’s such a relief. I wanted this so badly, and I worked so hard for it. And now, knowing that they’re actually going to move forward with it…” She trailed off, shaking her head with a laugh. “I’m just so freaking *happy*.”  

“You should be,” Tara said, reaching out to nudge Jodi’s arm. “And you deserve every second of this. So, what’s the plan? Celebratory drinks tonight? Fancy dinner? Or are you going home to make Jeff tell you how amazing you are for the rest of the evening?”  

Jodi laughed, covering her face with her hands. “Probably all of the above,” she admitted, her voice muffled. “But first, I need to actually get through the rest of the day without looking like I just won the lottery.”  

“Well, good luck with that,” Tara said, smirking. “You’ve been glowing all day, and now it’s like you’ve leveled up. People are going to start asking questions.”  

Jodi groaned, though her laughter betrayed her. “I can’t help it, okay? I’m just… really, really happy right now.”  

Tara stood, smoothing her skirt as she headed for the door. “Good. You should be. And hey, if you need me to run interference with anyone who asks why you’re grinning like a maniac, I’ve got you.”  

Jodi rolled her eyes, still smiling as Tara slipped out of the office. “Thanks, Tara,” she called after her.  

As the door closed behind her, Jodi leaned back in her chair, her smile widening as she let herself savor the moment. For the first time in a long time, everything felt *perfect.*    

---

Jodi couldn’t wait any longer. She grabbed her phone, her fingers flying across the screen as she texted Jeff:  

**Jodi:** *I crushed it.*  

A few seconds passed before the dots indicating Jeff was typing appeared, followed by his response:  

**Jeff:** *Of course you did. They’d have to be blind not to see how brilliant you are. Tell me EVERYTHING.*  

Jodi grinned, already feeling her heart swell from his support.  

**Jodi:** *The chairman said my plan was “exceptional.” He’s going to recommend it at the strategic review next week. Like, he personally said it. TO ME. This is HUGE.*  

**Jeff:** *Holy crap, babe, that’s incredible. I’m so proud of you! All that hard work paid off. You’re amazing.*  

Jodi blushed at the text, her smile growing even wider. She could practically hear his voice through the words, warm and full of excitement.  

**Jodi:** *Thank you. 🥹 I feel like I’m dreaming. I didn’t know if they’d go for it, but they DID.* 
**Jeff:** *Of course they did. They’d have to be insane to pass on your ideas. But the real question is… are they going to reward this brilliance financially? Because I see a raise in your future.*  

Jodi laughed, shaking her head as she typed back.  

**Jodi:** *I mean… they better. I didn’t just pull an all-nighter for fun. Let’s manifest that raise, okay?*  

**Jeff:** *Oh, we’re manifesting it. And celebrating it. Speaking of which… I’ve got a VERY important question.*  

Jodi raised an eyebrow, already anticipating the cheeky tone in his next message.  

**Jodi:** *Oh no. What?*  

**Jeff:** *How much did that video you sent me beforehand help? Because I’m pretty sure it gave you some kind of superpower.*  

Jodi burst out laughing, covering her face with one hand as she typed back with the other.  

**Jodi:** *You’re ridiculous. That was for YOU, not me.*  

**Jeff:** *Oh, trust me, it worked wonders for me. But you can’t tell me it didn’t give you a little confidence boost walking in there, knowing what you did.*  

**Jodi:** *Okay, fine. Maybe it helped a *little.* But I’m not giving the video ALL the credit!*  

**Jeff:** *Fair enough. You’re the genius here. But I’m still keeping the video on repeat all day to keep me motivated.*  

Jodi rolled her eyes, though her cheeks flushed as she thought about it.  

**Jodi:** *You’re impossible.*  

**Jeff:** *And yet, you’re madly in love with me.*  

**Jodi:** *I really, really am.*  

**Jeff:** *And I really, really can’t wait to hear more about your big win tonight. Celebratory dinner on me. You name the place.*  

Jodi smiled, her heart full as she quickly typed back:  

**Jodi:** *You’re on. And thanks for always being my biggest cheerleader.*  

**Jeff:** *Always, babe. You deserve all of it. Now go finish being the badass you are—I’ll see you tonight.*  

Jodi set her phone down, her chest warm with pride and love. She felt unstoppable, and knowing Jeff was just as proud of her as she was of herself made the moment even sweeter.    

---

When Jodi unlocked the door and stepped inside, the first thing she noticed was the aroma of sizzling steaks wafting through the air. Her stomach growled on cue—it had been a long day, and the promise of a perfectly cooked steak made her even more eager to kick off the celebration.  

But the second thing she noticed stopped her in her tracks: the living room TV.  

Her 106-second video was playing on a loop, front and center, in glorious high definition. The sound was mercifully muted, but the visuals were… unmistakable.  

Jodi’s jaw dropped, her bag slipping off her shoulder as she turned toward the kitchen, where Jeff was standing at the stove, expertly flipping steaks in a hot cast-iron skillet. He was wearing an apron—*her* apron, the one with the cheesy “Kiss the Cook” printed across the front—and looking entirely too casual for someone who had turned their living room into a private theater.  

“Jeff!” she exclaimed, pointing toward the TV with a mix of exasperation and disbelief. “What the hell?”  

Jeff glanced over his shoulder, a mischievous grin spreading across his face. “Oh, hey, babe. Welcome home!”  

“Don’t ‘welcome home’ me!” she said, though her laughter betrayed her. “You’ve got my—my *video*—on the TV? Really?”  

“Hey, I figured we should both enjoy your masterpiece,” Jeff replied, shrugging as he returned his attention to the steaks. “Besides, it’s motivational. It kept me focused while I cooked.”  

Jodi groaned, covering her face with one hand, though she couldn’t stop the smile creeping onto her lips. “You’re impossible,” she muttered, kicking off her heels and setting her bag on the counter.  

“You love me,” Jeff said, turning to give her a wink. “Now, can you grab me a cup of ice? I need it for my lemonade. Dinner’s almost ready.”  

Jodi shook her head, laughing as she walked to the freezer and pulled out the ice tray. “You know, most people would just play music or something while they cooked,” she teased, dropping a few cubes into a glass.  

“Most people don’t have a video like this in their possession,” Jeff shot back, his grin widening. “I mean, come on—it’s art.”  

“Art?” Jodi repeated, handing him the glass with a raised eyebrow. “You’re ridiculous.”  

“And yet, here you are,” he said, pouring lemonade into the glass before setting it on the counter.  

She sighed, leaning against the counter as she watched him plate the steaks with precision. A bowl of salad was already on the table, next to a chilled bottle of her favorite Chilean wine. It was clear Jeff had gone all out, and her heart swelled at the effort he’d put into celebrating her big day.  

“You’re lucky you’re cute,” she said, grabbing the wine and uncorking it.  

Jeff glanced up, his eyes softening as he looked at her. “No, you’re lucky you’re brilliant,” he said, his tone sincere now. “I’m so proud of you, Jodi. You deserve to be celebrated tonight—and every night.”  

Jodi felt her cheeks flush, a mix of pride and affection warming her chest. “Thanks,” she said softly, pouring herself a glass of wine. She took a sip, savoring the crisp flavor before adding, “But you’re still turning the TV off before we eat.”  

Jeff laughed, grabbing the remote and pausing the video. “Deal. But only because I want your undivided attention at dinner.”  

Jodi smiled, setting her glass down as she walked over to him, wrapping her arms around his waist. “You’ve got it,” she said, leaning up to kiss him.  

As the steaks rested and they set the table together, Jodi couldn’t help but think about how lucky she was—not just for her professional success, but for the man who always made her feel like the most extraordinary person in the room, no matter what.    

---  
Jodi sat back in her chair, a glass of her favorite Chilean wine in hand, as she watched Jeff across the table. He was cutting into his steak, his smile easy and warm, and every so often, he’d glance up at her with that look—the one that made her feel like she was the only person in the world. The table was set perfectly, with the salad bowl between them and the soft glow of candles Jeff had lit filling the room with a cozy ambiance.  

She took a sip of her wine and let her thoughts drift back over the day, a smile tugging at her lips as she replayed every moment.  

It had started before sunrise, as most Mondays did, but this one had been different. She’d woken up to Jeff’s warmth beside her, the way he held her close even as she stirred. She remembered waking him gently, fulfilling the promise she’d made the night before, and how completely, utterly consumed they’d been with each other in those quiet, early hours.  

Afterward, there’d been the mad scramble to get ready for work—her mismatched shoes, her half-buttoned shirt, and that rush out the door with just 30 seconds to spare. She grinned, thinking about Tara and Mike teasing her mercilessly in the break room, and how she’d laughed along with them, soaking in the camaraderie of her office family.  

And then there was *the* moment: her presentation.
